The 1-2-3 Formula: 2 Stories
The Dream Story

Okay this session deals with the Dream Story Formula.
Now, some people don’t think that they can use a nightmare story in their
business and, I think in the last section, we covered some incredibly creative
ways that you can.
Everyone – repeat, everyone – can use the Dream Story Formula. Everybody.
Now, again, we’re going to cover this. This is your Dream Story. And it should
be used throughout your video sales letter. Now, we’re going to tell you how to
use it throughout.
The nightmare story comes in one place; it’s just one long narrative. But this is
going to be used throughout your video sales letter. So, we’re going to tell you
how later on. Don’t worry about that. For now, let’s just get it going, let’s write it
down.
So, again, get out your Word document or on the Internet, however you want to
write this down. Let’s start writing and let’s get it written down. And then in the
later sessions, we will actually put this in PowerPoint and we’ll make it flow.
Okay?
So, here’s the formula for creating your dream story.
First, there’s follow the leader.
Next, there’s what we call the “specific you” and the “general they.” Now, that
sounds really weird, doesn’t it? Don’t worry, I’ll explain it. The transition phrase,
and the present tense promise.

-- 1 of 9 --

2
The good thing about the Dream Story Formula, it’s a lot shorter than the
Nightmare Story Formula. So, I think you will have no problems with this.
But this is absolutely essential. Again, our goal is what? To build rapport.
Building rapport does something very, very cool. We’re going to share that in this
session, what it does to the brain. And this is very important to selling anything.
Building rapport does something really unique to the brain. We’re going to cover
that.
And the Dream Story is building even more rapport with your reader, because
your Dream Story is, again, what their ideal life is going to look like once they
have your product in their hands or once they hire your services. What’s it going
to look like?
Well this is the formula: follow the leader, the specific you and the general they,
the transition phrase, and the present tense promise. Let’s dive into each one of
those.
First, we have follow the leader. Now, this is a simple transition into the dream
story. And this is what it does: it engages the readers right brain, the creative
part of your brain. This is the creative mind, and we want to engage it. And the
reason why we want to engage it is because it disengages the decision mode.
Don’t forget, decision is almost always logically-based, okay? So, we are
appealing to what? We’re appealing to emotions and we’re also appealing to
logic, but the logic comes in in the grand offer. Because at this point, the
emotions are engaged.
But this is what it does that’s so powerful: it temporarily disengages that decision
mode. It puts the reader into a desire mode.
If you remember from our previous session, our goal is to take the reader from
decision to desire, to actually longing for the service or product that you have.
And this is what follow the leader and the dream story does.
Now, here are some examples of very simple transition phrases.
“Imagine this.” I’ve used that phrase a thousand times, “imagine this,” and it still
works.
“Picture this for a moment.”
“Picture your life,” and then you explain what the ideal life of your ideal reader
would be.
And I love this one, “Walk with me for a moment, as we imagine.” Notice this
one’s very powerful because it is a “we” statement. “Walk with me for a moment
as we imagine you waking up tomorrow morning, knowing, with 100% certainty,
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 9 --

3
that you will have plenty of money in the bank, that your family is taken care of.”
This is their dream story.
But you say, “Walk with me for a moment,” because that takes it into a physical
realm. So, we’ve got physical and mental going on at once. It’s an advanced
technique. Don’t worry, we’ll cover the advanced techniques in the last session.
I’m just throwing them in a little bit here at a time, so the advanced writers go,
“Oh,” they get that.
“So, walk with me for a moment as we imagine.” It’s an NLP (Neuro-Linguistic
Programming) trick. And I’m basically saying “physical/mental.” They’re
dichotomies, right? “Walk with me for a moment as we imagine.” So, that’s
pretty cool.
“Imagine this” works. I use that all the time. So, it’s not like you have to get so
fancy, otherwise it won’t work. “Imagine this.” This is what you want your reader
to do.
When you say, “Imagine this,” all of a sudden, you can watch this in studies that
we’ve done in psychology, and looking at peoples eyes and were they look when
you say, “I want you to imagine something for a moment,” and you pause.
Invariably, invariably that right brain just kids in.
They’re going, “Okay, I’ve got to get into imagination mode. I can’t be logical.”
It’s very difficult to be logical when you’ve been asked to imagine something.
Again, we’ll talk about where to put this in your video sales letter in another
session. Don’t worry about that right now, we just need to get it written. So,
those are some examples.
The next up is the specific you and the general they. This is another exercise
that you are going to do about yourself. And I’ll tell you this right now, this is
absolutely one of the biggest mistakes I see marketers make, and we’re going to
give some pretty funny examples of this in a moment.
But, I want you to start by asking yourself this question: what is an example of
your #1 USP benefit, whatever that is, and how does that benefit play out in your
life, using a specific example?
So, taking something very specific. How does your #1 benefit, because I assume
that your #1 benefit is something that you experience, otherwise you wouldn’t be
an expert in the field and you wouldn’t be writing a video sales letter about it, how
does this #1 benefit play out in your life? And get as specific as you want.
Now, I’m going to give you some examples here.
Here’s one of mine: “Waking up knowing that I’m going to eat a pepperoni pizza
for dinner tonight, with my favorite girl, as we curl up and watch a football game.”
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 9 --

4
Now, that is Jon’s specific me. That’s specific to me. If this was a lifestyle
program, I could say, “I can wake up at 10:00 in the morning, or whenever I want
to get up.” But it’s not. This is a diet. So I’d say, “Waking up knowing that I can
eat a pepperoni pizza later, that’s kind of cool, and still lose weight.”
So, obviously, this is in the context of weight loss, otherwise this would not make
any sense.
I chose my favorite girl, so if I have a girlfriend or I’m on a date, it’s my favorite
girl, we curl up and we watch a football game, I love football.
Now, what’s wrong with this picture?
“I’m going to show you another one. Driving my Ferrari to my private plane, as I
take my hot model girlfriend on a 2-week vacation to Maui.”
In case you’re wondering, that is not my specific me. I don’t drive a Ferrari, I
don’t have a private plan. I won’t talk about the other 2.
I see this all the time, especially with IM guys that have a finance product, they
have a make money product, a biz-op product, etc, etc. They’re always going,
“Look, this is me standing next to my 16 Lamborghini’s, 8 models, all in bikinis,
against my mansion.” And your reader’s going, “Like I can freaking do that! Are
you nuts? I can barely pay tomorrow’s bills. I can’t pay the next month’s rent. I
don’t care that you have a freaking Ferrari or a Lamborghini or whatever, I don’t
care. In fact, it kind of offends me.”
Yet, you see this all over the Internet. You see it all over the Internet. And the
only people that fall for it are the saps. I’m telling you, you don’t want to cater to
the saps, you want your clients to be the savvy. There’s the savvy and the saps.
I love savvy. And the people that I cater to and the products that I develop,
they’re basically for people who can think.
So, that’s me. And if you want the savvier clients, because the savvier clients
buy more, refund less, appreciate what you do more, that’s huge. Like you guys
have shown a great deal of appreciation today. And I tell you, it’s the best form
of client to have, is this client.
That is what you see all the time. And the problem with this is rather obvious.
This is not their dream, this is your dream. Your dream. So, do not assume that
your dreams are your readers’ dreams. I think that that makes plenty of sense.
Maybe later, by the way, in the video sales letter, check out what I was able to do
with my Dream Life. Later on, maybe you show a picture of one thing, to make it
kind of that you say, “Well, I’ve got a really cool house.” Great. Don’t overload
them.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 9 --

5
But, what you want to do is what are your readers’ dreams? Understand that
their dreams may be something as simple as, “I want to make a couple of
thousand dollars a month extra, because I am suffering.” Again, I’m using
finances as an example, it works with any industry. What does your client want?
I’ll go back to something like document shredding, for example. They want
peace of mind. People want peace of mind in that.
If you are a hospital administer, again they want to know that their hospital’s
running effectively and efficiently, not wasting money on fill-in-the-blank. 	So,
what is their dream? Walk them through their dream. Walk them through their
dream.
Remember, their dream is not your dream. Their dream is not your dream.
So, the solution to this is that you want to take their general they statement and
you want to make it into something that all of your readers can relate to.
So, how do we do that? Well, it’s easier than you think.
I’m going to use 2 personal examples from the previous slide, the ones that I
used a while ago, to show you.
“Waking up knowing that you’re going to eat your absolute favorite food for
dinner tonight, whatever that may be, and maybe even enjoy it with your best
friend.” Do you see how that took what I said, “I’m going to wake up knowing that
I’m going to eat pepperoni pizza with my girlfriend and watch football.”
Well what if they don’t have a girlfriend? What if they’re married? What if they’re
gay? What if they hate football? What if they hate pizza? You can’t assume that
your dreams are their dreams. I see it all the time.
So, what we do is we create the general they. Take your dream, which you have
to know first, by the way. You have to get your dreams down.
And then, take that to a general state. So, here’s what I’ve done. I’ve changed
the food to something very specific: your absolute favorite food. How many
times do you think that you should use the word “you” and “your” in your video
sales letter? Ah, about 1,000. You really can’t use it too much.
So, I’m plugging it in everywhere I go; because, again, I’m speaking to one
person. I’m not speaking to an audience, I’m speaking to one person.
So, “Waking up knowing that you are going to eat your favorite, absolute favorite
food for dinner tonight, whatever that may be,” that’s completely open, that’s
completely general. It may be tofu, for all you know. Some people may like tofu.
I certainly don’t. “And enjoy it.” I even go so far as to say, “And maybe even
enjoy it with your best friend.” So that’s really open, because their best friend
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 9 --

6
may be a dog, their best friend may be their wife, or husband, their best friend
may be a best pal. They may not have a best friend.
So you say, “Maybe even enjoy it...” so if they’re going, “I wish I had a best
friend,” it will only cross their mind for a second. Now I don’t know of very many
people that will have no best friends at all. But this is how powerful this exercise
is. When you take things to the general they, you will relate to mass amounts of
people, rather than specifics. Like Ferrari’s, going to Maui, that kind of thing.
So, let’s take the Ferrari vacation example. “You’ll feel absolute financial
freedom as you dart off to your favorite vacation spot with your ideal travel
partner.”
You see how open that is? It’s completely general. But I’m telling them what
they’re going to feel. That’s very important. In this case, they’re going to feel
financial freedom. I could actually use that freedom bit in the very first one.
“Waking up knowing that you’re going to enjoy the freedom of eating absolutely
your favorite food.” You see how that works?
People crave freedom, please write that down. People crave freedom. Please
write that down. People crave freedom. And the more your video sales letter
can relate to freedom, it doesn’t matter what industry you’re in, it does not matter
if it’s the most bland-sounding thing on earth or if it’s the sexiest-sounding thing
on earth, freedom is what every human being wants.
And the more you can relate to freedom, the more desire you will build. Freedom
is essential. So, “You will feel absolute financial freedom, as you dart off to your
favorite vacation spot.” Who knows what it is? It could be Alaska. Maybe
people hate Maui, I don’t know why people would hate Maui, but maybe they do.
“With your ideal travel partner.” Your travel partner may be a bird. It may not be
a person at all.
So, you don’t want to say “with your mate,” “Your ideal travel partner.” Get as
general as you can. That’s how you start the Dream Story.
Then, you have a Transition Phrase. This transitions the reader from the
hypothetical to the immediately applicable. So, we’re taking it out of some sort of
hypothetical realm and we’re temporarily engaging that left brain again, and
we’re going to make something immediately applicable for them.
Working on both hemispheres of the brain, people are not all right brain or all left
brain, it’s across the mix. Yet, I’ll read sales letter after sales letter that caters
only to one.
You’re going to cater to everyone in the world, and that’s the goal. Every single
person you can. That’s what makes this whole magic work.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 9 --

7
Here’s some examples of what I mean.
“You know what the greatest feeling you’ll have is?” Or, “You want to know what
the great feeling you’re going to experience is? You know what the best part of
this whole thing is going to be?” Or, “Here’s the best part: you’re going to blank,”
or “you will blank.”
See, I’m taking what they’re experiencing, I’m taking the imagine and I’m saying,
“You know what the best part of that is?” And they’re going like, “Yeah, I think I
do.” But you’re going to follow that with what’s called a present tense promise.
So, you’re going to make a promise in the present. You’re actually imagining the
future.
And again, that is called future pacing. That’s another psychological/NLP term,
“future pacing.” You’re walking someone into a future, assuming that it’s already
happening. But now, you want to make a present tense promise to bring them
back into the now. And here’s how you do it.
“Tonight, you’re going to be able to walk into a restaurant,” etc. “That’s the best
thing about this. So, imagine being able to know that you’re going to wake up
and you’re going to enjoy your favorite food, your favorite food with maybe your
best friend. And do you know what the best thing about that is? Tonight, you’re
going to be able to walk into any restaurant and order whatever you want.”
So, I’ve taken it from an imagine to tonight. And you’ll notice a pattern here.
“Within a month, you’ll be able to walk into any car dealership,” notice I don’t say
walk into a Ferrari dealership, “Walk into any car dealership, pick the one you
want, never have to worry about it.”
“Tomorrow, you will go to any bar and be able to pick up any girl in the joint,” for
you dating guys who are on. “Tomorrow. The best part about what I’ve just
asked you to imagine is that today or tomorrow or tonight.” What does this have
in common? Even within a month.
It’s an NLP anchor to something that is almost immediate. In this case, it
anchors the reader’s feelings to a specific date and time. And that’s usually
today or tomorrow or tonight. I kind of like tonight because it’s just kind of a cool
word, and a lot of people like nighttime. So, “Tonight,” “You’ll be able to do this
today.” You can say, “Immediately,” but I like to actually name the time period.
And here’s what that does. That creates an urgency to act, you’ve embedded an
anchor. You’ve embedded an anchor. And what I mean by that is you’ve said,
“Here’s the life that I want you to imagine. Imagine it with me. Imagine with me,
now. Do you know that the best part is? It’s today,” and what I’ve done when I
say, “Today,” I kick-start somebody right back into the present. “Today, you’ll be
able to put this to use.”
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 9 --

8
Today, and I’ll give them a specific example. “Today you’ll be able to walk into
any bank in the United States and get a loan.” if you sell a loan-getting product.
And maybe if it’s not today, you say tomorrow or next week. Something soon.
That creates an urgency to act.
Because when I say “tomorrow,” that’s urgent. When I say “today,” that’s super
urgent. So if you’re product can deliver something immediately, you definitely
want to emphasize what it can deliver immediately, and do so in a very powerful
way.
When you use this technique, you’ve temporarily disengaged the left brain,
you’ve engaged the right brain, so they’re in an imagination state. They’re in a
creative state, what makes the creative state so powerful is it disengages
decision-making, it engages desire. That is our goal, remember?
Our goal is to engage desire; to get your user to a state from decision to desire.
You’re doing this subconsciously, using this NLP anchor, and then you bring it
home by saying, “Tonight, this is going to happen,” or “Tomorrow, this is going to
happen.”
I even say, “Within a month.” That still creates urgency, because if they’re going
to start, they have to get started now to get to that month. So, it creates an
urgency to act.
Now, I like to say, “Better than within a month, by the way.” It’s within 28 days.
Guys, write this tip down. You’re going to want to write this tip down, because a
specific number is an awesome anchor point. “Within 28 days from today, you
will be able to walk into any car dealership,” I bet I have your attention, even if
you don’t want to buy a car now.
It’s telling somebody 28 days. So, yes, get very, very specific. That’s not on the
slide here, but it should be. Get very specific with this. Get a number, or say,
“Today or tomorrow.”
Numbers are awesome. Numbers are magical. When you use numbers in your
video sales letters and in your headlines too, by the way, if you have a headline
above your sales letter, “7 ways to blank,” it’s just a magical thing. You see it on
every magazine, every magazine that sells a lot. You see it on all the top-
converting sales letters. But using numbers within this, as a present-tense
promise, 6 days from today, what have I done? When I say, “You know what the
best part of all this is? Is that 7 days from this very moment, you’re going to
be...” What has that done?
That has put the person into 2 mental positions. It’s taken them into the present
tense, today, within 7 days, notice I used both words. “Imagine 7 days from
today, 27 days from today, you will be able to go into any car dealership.” What
does that assume? That assumes that they are going to take action today.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

9
So, you’ve already implanted a command for them to take action today. You’re
instilling desire for them to have an urgency to act.
